channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: The Lost Wild May Be the Dinosaur Horror Game You're Looking For
item.content:encoded:
You are part of the food chain in The Lost Wild. We had the chance to see a thirty minute demo, which was incredibly tense and showed the terrifying reality of what it would be like with dinosaurs around.

It was made clear early and stated several times that The Lost Wild is more of a tense cat and mouse game, not one in which you're going to be fighting dinosaurs. You're there to observe, adapt, and survive.

The tone is set early when in the demo we ran across some sort of researcher in a hazmat suit that gets spooked and runs right into a dinosaur. The dinosaur promptly picked the person up, chomped them in half, and the gory half of their body left hit the ground in front of us as we hid under a van. The dinosaur then picked it up and walked off.

Much of the demo was spent hidden in the brush observing and listening for the dinosaur that we had just seen eat someone. Every so often the loud thuds of it stomping in the forest could be heard, and most of the time we didn't see the dinosaur. Each stop was ominous and immediately upped the tension.

From the moment we saw the dinosaur, the feeling of the demo switched. Heart rate went up a bit, we were on alert, and all the classic markers of a horror game were present. In this case though, the big monster is a dinosaur we know can snap us up without much effort.

Developer Great Ape Games emphasized that this is just what dinosaurs would behave like, they're not trying to videogameify the dinosaurs in some way but just to naturally portray the fact that they're predators and you're prey.

While we didn't see them, you will eventually get some tools to "fight back." They didn't offer details, however.

All we really saw was the ability to pick up and throw the standard objects like glass bottles to make a sound and distract the dinosaur. Like, say, if you have a keycard and need to get it into a machine but need the dinosaur far enough away.

To up the more classic horror feel, you can only manually save if you have a save disk on you. They are found throughout the world and the machines to slot them in are scattered as well. They made sure to note that the fact you start putting in a disk isn't enough to save you, as there's a bit of a process and animation before the saving occurs. So, you'll need to be relatively safe, and it won't save you at the last second. Plan to die frequently.

As far as a demo presentation goes, The Lost Wild was great. The tension, the fear permeating from the screen, the overwhelming sounds… it accomplished its goal in making for a stressful experience.

With how common fodder dinosaurs are as enemies in games, it is refreshing to see them as truly terrifying as they actually would be if encountered in real life.

Survival horror fans should keep a close eye on The Lost Wild, especially if you're tired of the zombie aesthetic.

Andrew is the Editor in Chief at TechRaptor. Conned into a love of gaming by Nintendo at a young age, Andrew has been chasing the dragon spawned by… More about Andrew
